title: Scope and Closures Module Knowledge Check
description: A tricky conceptual quiz covering scope, hoisting, closures, closure scope, higher-order functions, callbacks, and returned functions.
author: DailyCoder

questions.explanation: JavaScript uses lexical scope. `showValue` was written in the global scope, so it uses the global `value`, not the local `value` from where it is called.
`const` is block-scoped, so `message` only exists inside the `if` block.
`var` is not block-scoped. In this top-level example, `status` is still accessible after the block.
The inner `const level` shadows the outer one for the whole block, but it is in the temporal dead zone until its declaration line.
The `var name` declaration is hoisted and initialized to `undefined`; the assignment to `"Ava"` happens later.

questions.prompt: Which statement about hoisting is most accurate?

options.text: JavaScript physically moves all declarations to the top of the file
Declarations are prepared before execution, but assignments still happen where written

questions.explanation: Hoisting describes JavaScript's setup phase for declarations. It does not physically rewrite your code, and assignments are not moved.
`run` is a `const` variable in the temporal dead zone until the declaration line. The function assignment has not happened yet.
Function declarations are fully hoisted within their scope, so `run` can be called before its declaration appears.

options.text: It throws a `ReferenceError` because `outer` has finished

questions.explanation: The returned function closes over `secret`, so the variable remains accessible through the closure after `outer` returns.
Both returned methods close over the same `count` variable from the same call to `createCounter`.
Each call to `createCounter()` creates a separate closure environment, so `a` and `b` have independent `count` variables.
All callbacks share the same function-scoped `var i`. By the time the timers run, the loop has finished and `i` is `3`.
`let` creates a fresh binding for each loop iteration, so each callback closes over its own `i`.

options.text: Returning `state` exposes the same object reference to outside code

questions.explanation: The variable is private, but returning the object directly exposes the same object reference. Outside code can mutate it.
The returned function remembers `multiplier` as `2`, so `double(7)` returns `14`.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `handleClick` and `handleClick()` when passing a callback?

options.text: `handleClick` passes the function; `handleClick()` calls it immediately and passes its return value

questions.explanation: Without parentheses, you pass the function reference. With parentheses, you execute the function immediately.

questions.prompt: Which callback likely runs asynchronously?
questions.explanation: `setTimeout` schedules its callback to run later. Array method callbacks usually run synchronously during the method call.
With braces, an arrow function needs an explicit `return`. The callback returns `undefined` each time, so no items pass the filter.
`curriedAdd(5)` returns a function that remembers `a` as `5`; calling that returned function with `3` returns `8`.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects closures and higher-order functions?

options.text: When a higher-order function returns a function, the returned function can close over the outer function's variables
Closures only work inside event listeners

questions.explanation: Returned functions often rely on closures to remember configuration, private state, or arguments from the outer function.
